question: Strontium ($Z = 38$) is an alkaline-earth metal with a two-valence-electron structure (like Ca and Yb). It has four stable isotopes: $^{84}$Sr, $^{86}$Sr, $^{87}$Sr (fermion, $I=9/2$), $^{88}$Sr (boson, most abundant). The primary cooling transition is:

options: $^1S_0 \to ^1P_1$ at 461 nm (blue, $\Gamma/2\pi = 32$ MHz) for initial cooling; $^1S_0 \to ^3P_1$ at 689 nm (red, $\Gamma/2\pi = 7.5$ kHz) for narrow-line Doppler cooling to $\mu$K
The primary transition is at 780 nm, identical to Rb
Sr has only one cooling transition at 461 nm
The 689 nm transition has $\Gamma/2\pi = 6$ MHz (same as Rb D2)
passive.explanation: Two-stage cooling: (1) Blue MOT on $^1S_0 \to ^1P_1$ (461 nm) cools to $\sim 1$ mK but leaks population to metastable $^3P_2$ via $^1D_2$; repumpers at 707 nm and 679 nm are needed. (2) Red MOT on $^1S_0 \to ^3P_1$ (689 nm, intercombination line): $\Gamma/2\pi = 7.5$ kHz gives Doppler limit $T_D = \hbar\Gamma/2k_B \approx 180$ nK — 100$\times$ colder than possible with a typical alkali MOT. Red MOTs reach $\sim 1{-}2\,\mu$K.

active.prompt: Match each Sr transition to its properties:

pairs.definition: Broad: $\Gamma/2\pi = 32$ MHz; strong initial cooling; Doppler limit 770 $\mu$K; leaks to $^3P_2$
Narrow intercombination: $\Gamma/2\pi = 7.5$ kHz; Doppler limit 180 nK; used for red MOT
Ultra-narrow: $\Gamma/2\pi \approx 1$ mHz; $\tau \approx 160$ s; world's best optical clock transition ($Q \sim 10^{17}$)
10-fold $m_F$ degeneracy; SU(10) symmetric Fermi gas in magic-wavelength lattice; analogue of nuclear matter

active.explanation: The Sr $^3P_0$ clock transition (698 nm, $Q \sim 10^{17}$) is the basis for the world's most precise optical lattice clocks, surpassing Cs fountain clocks by orders of magnitude. The magic wavelength (813 nm) for the clock transition cancels lattice-induced light shifts, enabling $10^{-19}$ fractional accuracy.

question: The Sr optical lattice clock uses the $^1S_0 \to ^3P_0$ transition in $^{87}$Sr trapped in a 1D optical lattice at the 'magic wavelength' $\lambda_m = 813$ nm. The magic wavelength condition is:

options: At $\lambda_m$, the AC Stark shifts of the $^1S_0$ and $^3P_0$ levels are equal — the differential light shift (which would shift the clock frequency) cancels to first order for all atoms regardless of their location in the lattice
The magic wavelength cancels the Zeeman shift from stray magnetic fields
At $\lambda_m$, photon scattering from the lattice laser is zero
The magic wavelength condition requires circularly polarised lattice light
passive.explanation: The differential AC Stark shift $\delta\nu = (\alpha_{^3P_0} - \alpha_{^1S_0})I/2\varepsilon_0 c$ is zero at $\lambda_m = 813$ nm where the dynamic polarisabilities $\alpha$ of the two states are equal. Atoms at different lattice sites see different intensities but all have the same (zero) differential shift — the 'Lamb-Dicke' regime also suppresses Doppler and recoil shifts. Current Sr lattice clocks achieve systematic uncertainties of $10^{-18}$–$10^{-19}$, sensitive enough to measure the gravitational redshift over 1 cm height differences.

question: $^{87}$Sr has nuclear spin $I = 9/2$, giving 10 magnetic sublevels $m_F = -9/2, -7/2, \ldots, +9/2$ in the ground state. In a magic-wavelength optical lattice, this creates an SU(10) symmetric Fermi gas:

options: The SU($N$) symmetry with $N = 2I+1 = 10$ arises because the lattice and interactions do not mix the nuclear spin states — this is the largest SU($N$) fermionic system realised, analogous to QCD SU(3) colour symmetry but more tractable
SU(10) symmetry requires equal populations of all 10 sublevels
The SU(10) symmetry is broken by the magnetic field used to address the clock transition
The nuclear spin makes $^{87}$Sr behave like a boson in optical lattices
passive.explanation: Because the two-body interaction (van der Waals, contact) is insensitive to nuclear spin in the ground state, and the clock photon couples to nuclear spin independently of orbital degrees of freedom, the SU(10) symmetry is nearly exact. The Kondo effect, SU($N$) Hubbard model, chiral spin liquids, and colour superfluidity have been explored using this system. First realised by the Ye group at JILA (2014). BEC of $^{84}$Sr (the bosonic isotope with near-zero scattering length that can be tuned) was demonstrated in 2009.
